title: Monolithically Integrated VO$_2$ Mott Oscillators for Energy-Efficient Spiking Neurons
authors: Fabio Bersano, Cyrille Masserey, Vanessa Conti, Andrea Iaconeta, Niccolo' Martinolli, Ehsan Ansari, Anna Varini, Igor Stolichnov, Adrian Mihai Ionescu
abstract: Brain-inspired non-Boolean computing offers intrinsic error tolerance and parallelism, but its practical deployment is limited by the lack of compact, energy-efficient spiking hardware compatible with large-scale integration. Mott phase-transition materials provide a promising route, as their abrupt insulator-to-metal transitions enable neuron-like thresholding and oscillatory dynamics in compact devices. Among these, vanadium dioxide (VO$_2$) stands out for its near-room-temperature transition, fast switching, and scalability. However, existing VO$_2$-based neuristors rely on discrete components, limiting integration density and system applicability. Here, we report monolithic back-end-of-the-line (BEOL) integration of one-transistor-one-VO2-memristor (1T-1MR) spiking neurons on CMOS-compatible platforms. VO$_2$ nanosheet devices are fabricated by pulsed-laser deposition below 430 °C on dielectrically isolated silicon-on-insulator (SOI) p-type junctionless field-effect transistors (JLFETs) in a compact 1T-1MR configuration. The architecture exhibits gate-tunable oscillations from 40 to 410 kHz in 60 nm-thick VO$_2$ devices with an active area of 6 $μ$m$^2$, achieving energy consumption as low as 18 pJ per spike at room temperature, with memristor power dissipation of 8 $μ$W and potential scaling toward sub-3 $μ$W operation. We further uncover a non-monotonic dependence of oscillation frequency on current and temperature, along with bias-dependent stochastic firing dynamics, highlighting the rich behavior of integrated VO$_2$ memristor systems. Finally, we demonstrate voltage-controlled oscillator functionality and actively tunable resistive coupling of two nano-oscillators mediated by a JLFET. These results establish a pathway toward dense, energy-efficient, and monolithically integrated Mott-based neuromorphic hardware compatible with CMOS technology.
summary: This paper reports the successful monolithic integration of VO2-based Mott oscillators with CMOS-compatible SOI transistors. By using a 3D integration technique, the authors created a highly efficient 1T-1MR spiking neuron architecture. The device demonstrates gate-tunable oscillations and extremely low energy consumption per spike. The work also provides a detailed analysis of the stochastic dynamics and thermal effects governing the insulator-to-metal transition, paving the way for dense, energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware.
main_problem: The lack of compact, energy-efficient, and monolithically integrated hardware for large-scale neuromorphic computing, specifically the difficulty in integrating VO2-based memristors with CMOS technology.
main_result: Demonstrated the first 3D monolithic back-end-of-the-line (BEOL) integration of VO2-based 1T-1MR spiking neurons on SOI JLFETs, achieving gate-tunable oscillations and energy consumption as low as 18 pJ per spike.
method: The researchers used pulsed-laser deposition (PLD) for VO2 growth and ion beam etching for patterning, combined with 3D COMSOL Multiphysics simulations and analytical modeling of the Mott insulator-to-metal transition dynamics.
model_or_system: A 3D heterogeneous integration of VO2 nanosheet memristors and p-type Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI) junctionless field-effect transistors (JLFETs) in a 1T-1MR architecture.
key_observables: Oscillation frequency, energy consumption per spike, threshold voltage (Vth), holding voltage (Vhl), and spike-rate encoding efficiency.
important_parameters: Oscillation frequency (40 to 410 kHz), energy per spike (18 pJ), thermal budget (< 430 C), and VO2 thickness (60 nm).
main_assumptions: The analytical model assumes an affine-function approximation for nonlinear I-V characteristics and uses a thermal RC model as a first-order approximation for Joule heating.
figures_summary: The figures show the 3D integration schematic, fabrication steps, SEM/TEM structural characterization, gate-tunable oscillation waveforms, temperature/bias dependence of frequency, and the demonstration of synchronized oscillators and spike-rate encoding.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the motivation for neuromorphic hardware, moves to the fabrication and structural characterization of the integrated 1T-1MR device, presents the electrical characterization of the oscillations and their tunability, provides an analytical and thermal model for the dynamics and stochasticity, and concludes with the demonstration of functional neuromorphic primitives like synchronization and encoding.
why_interesting: While primarily an engineering paper, the study of the stochastic transition between deterministic and noise-driven spiking regimes and the modeling of non-equilibrium phase transitions in a driven system is relevant to researchers studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Schematic of the spiking neuron comprising a VO2 memristor and a JLFET, with corresponding material characterization. a 3D-model of the proposed integration scheme and equivalent circuit schematic. VO2 two-terminal devices are connected to the JL transistor through metal-filled through-oxide-vias (TOV). b SEM image of the fabricated chip, showing multiple JLFETs connected to the VO2 node to enable IMT operation at the target current level. D, S, and G denote the drain, source, and gate terminals, respectively. c STEM-EDX and BF TEM images of the gate stack of the JLFETs after VO2 integration. d SEM top view of a VO2 nanosheet device. The blue line represents the direction of the line...

Fig. 2 Electrical characterization of JLFETs and VO2 nanosheet memristors. a, b Transfer and output characteristics of a JLFET prior to VO2 integration. c, d Transfer and output characteristics of a JLFET after VO2 integration. The inset in (c) shows the subthreshold slope as a function of the normalized drain current. The channel width and length are W = 30 µm and L = 2 µm, the Si substrate is at 0 V. e Example of I-V characteristic for a VO2 nanosheet device together with the indication of the upper threshold voltage (VT H) and current (IT H). The darker curve is the average of 30 consecutive measurements (in light blue) obtained with a voltage step of 10 mV. The histogram of VT H is...

Fig. 3 Self oscillations and frequency histogram of a fully integrated artificial neuron. a Measured waveform at room temperature, showing a minimum VO2 energy consumption of ≈18 pJ per spike (Methods). The dashed line represents the oscillation reproduced by the analytical model in Eq. 2. b Circuit schematic of the device, including the capacitive (CL ≈70 pF) and resistive (RL ≈1 MΩ) loads given by the oscilloscope. c Histogram of the oscillation frequency corresponding to the measurement in a, obtained over more than 200 cycles. The peak frequency is 410 kHz, with a standard deviation of σ = 18.8 kHz.

Fig. 4 Dependence of integrated VO2 oscillators on device temperature and bias current. a Measured waveforms at different substrate temperatures under a constant bias current of 21.46 µA. b-c Evolution of the measured VO2 threshold voltage as a function of bias current and substrate temperature. The values in (b) and the red dots in (c) are extracted from voltage oscillations, while the blue crosses correspond to the average of 30 I-V measurements the dotted line shows a linear fit used for the analytical model. d-e Evolution of the holding voltage as a function of bias current and substrate temperature; the legend is the same as in (b-c). f Extracted oscillation frequency at different...

Fig. 5 Analysis of firing stochasticity in integrated VO2 spiking neurons. a Representative spike cycle for a circuit operating within the oscillation regime. b Representative spike cycle for a circuit operating outside the analytically defined holding- voltage oscillation condition. c Close-up view of (a). d Close-up view of (b). e Falling escape time as a function of the hold voltage margin (each point represents the median over at least 1000 cycles). Inset: rising escape time as a function of the threshold voltage margin for a different device (median over at least 200 cycles). f–h Falling escape time histograms for different holding voltage margins, corresponding to the points...

Fig. 6 Dynamic operation of an integrated VO2 voltage-controlled oscillator. a Circuit schematic of the integrated device, including the external voltage function generator and the equivalent load representing the oscilloscope channel. The transistor is an SOI p-type JLFET operating in depletion mode, while the load resistance and capacitance are RL=1 MΩand CL≈70 pF, respectively. b Static I-V characteristic of the device under test, showing 30 cycles measured at 25 ◦C. The light-blue area indicates the bias-current range swept by the transistor under gate-voltage control. c,d Measurements obtained by applying a linear ramp and a sinusoidal voltage signal to the gate, respectively, showing...

Fig. 7 On-chip active resistive coupling of integrated VO2 oscillators. a Circuit schematic of two 3D-integrated VO2 oscillators (A and B) coupled via an on-chip depletion-mode JLFET. The schematic includes the equivalent loads corresponding to the two oscilloscope channels, with RL = 1 MΩand CL ≈70 pF. b Static I-V characteristics of the devices under test, measured at 25 ◦C. c Measured waveforms over a time scale of 5 ms, along with the extracted frequency (raw data and moving average) for both channels. The average frequency for the two channels is 43 kHz, with a standard deviation of ∼13 kHz and ∼15 kHz. d Zoom-in of the measured waveforms over a time scale of 200 µs, showing in-phase...

Fig. 8 PLD thermal budget. Temperature profile measured during PLD deposition (ramp up, deposition and ramp down). The baseline temperature was set to 400 °C and the maximum temperature approaches 430 °C.
Fig. 9 Etching of VO2 thin films. Schematic illustration of the three IBE steps used for patterning VO2 devices on top of SOI JLFETs.

Fig. 8 shows the temperature profile extracted from the log-file of the PLD machine, with a peak tempera- ture not exceeding 430 °C. The sequence of the IBE steps to fabricate the VO2 two-terminal devices is reported in Fig. 9: after deposition the film is thinned from 100 nm to 60 nm (IBE 1), then VO2 is patterned to leave a 60 nm thick layer with a rectangular footprint, Ac = WcLc (IBE 2). In the final step, the active area (device channel) is defined by further thinning the surrounding VO2.
